text: Welcome to another installment of robotic hernia surgery. Today we're talking about robotic transabdominal transversus abdominis release. This is a case of a forty-five-year-old woman with multiple surgical interventions who presented with a chronically incarcerated ventral hernia.

Her index operation was a laparoscopic cholecystectomy which was complicated by a misdenturotomy requiring emergency laparotomy and washout. She had a BMI of forty-two at initial consultation. We worked towards optimization with weight loss, but unfortunately, given the nature of her hernia and the pain, we didn't achieve much.

This is her CT scan, multifocal midline incisional hernia, M one through three with dimensions of eight by twenty-three. It extended very close to the xiphoid, about one and a half centimeters from the xiphoid on the CT scan.

So in terms of setup, we knew that we were most likely going to need to do a subdiaphragmatic dissection. And in these scenarios, I have a low threshold for performing a transversus abdominis release.

The surgical team begins on the left side, docking the robotic system laterally. The visual field reveals a multifocal midline incisional hernia with Swiss cheese-like defects, including several more prominent defects in the lower region.
The surgeon addresses extensive adhesions throughout the midline and extending laterally, involving the omentum, stomach, and colon, which are closely adhered to the abdominal wall.

No major difficulties with lysis but definitely a scenario that I I think an ETAP or extraperitoneal approach could pose some danger. So transabdominal here I think worked out well given the nature of her adhesive disease.

The video shows quick shots of adhesions being taken down from the abdominal wall. The surgeon initiates the retromuscular operation by incising around the linear elbow to enter the rectal rectus space on the patient's right side. The hernia extends to the umbilicus, eliminating the need for dissection into the space of Retzius.
The camera pans upward, revealing the hernia extending superiorly to the xiphoid process. The extent of the hernia is visually confirmed, indicating the need for a subdiaphragmatic dissection.

The surgeon observes the absence of a prominent falciform ligament, likely altered during a previous emergency operation. After incising into the posterior sheath, the dissection is extended laterally towards the semilunar line.

The surgeon performs a robotic twin-versus-abdominance release, initiating a bottoms-up approach. The procedure involves making the cave and incising the sheath medial to the semilunar line, addressing tension in the abdominal area.

The surgeon performs a preperitoneal dissection, reflecting the transversalis fascia along with the transversus abdominis. After completing a significant portion of this dissection, the approach transitions to a top-down TAR (transversus abdominis release), unifying the dissections from the inferior and superior aspects.

The surgeon incises the posterior lamella of the internal abdominal oblique and transects the transversus abdominis, continuing the dissection down to the preperitoneal plane. The prominent transversus or transversalis fascia is visible, and the surgical planes are being unified.

The surgeon reflects the muscle while maintaining the transversalis fascia during the posterior dissection, ensuring it remains intact to minimize fenestrations. The dissection occurs in a different plane from the inferior dissection, with plans to eventually transect the transversalis fascia and unify the dissections.

The surgeon demonstrates subdiaphragmatic dissection, extending the dissection to achieve adequate mesh coverage over the superior defect. Muscle fibers are oriented upwards during this process. After completing the dissection, the surgical team redocks on the patient's right side.

The surgeon performs a contralateral dissection, incising into the retro rectus space and preparing for posterior and anterior closure. The linea alba is identified, and mesh placement is planned. The patient's healthy abdominal musculature is visible, facilitating the procedure.

The surgeon demonstrates a bottoms-up approach by incising the posterior sheath and entering the preperitoneal space. The transversalis fascia is reflected upwards with the transversus abdominis, focusing on the tendinous portion. A trocar from the contralateral side becomes visible. The surgeon transitions upwards, incising the posterior lamella of the internal abdominal oblique aponeurosis to reveal and transect the transversus abdominis, unifying the dissection. The diaphragm is visible, indicating the subdiaphragmatic dissection.

The surgeon demonstrates the lateral dissection, highlighting a slight difference in the dissection plane to achieve a less bloody approach. The dissection progresses into the pre-transversalis plane, aiming to expose the retroperitoneal fat. The procedure is finalized to ensure a proper release and adequate space for placing a large piece of mesh.

The surgeon observes the absence of midline hernias and notes the subdiaphragmatic dissection on the patient's left hemidiaphragm, identifying a weakness and defect. Mesh is used to cover the defect without closing it. Closure of the linea alba is initiated using an OV lock suture, with sequential tightening to plicate and close hernia defects. The insufflation pressure is set to twelve, and the Transversus abdominis release aids in reducing tension during closure, extending to the xiphoid.

The surgeon closes the posterior visceral sac using a three absorbable V-lock suture, addressing fenestrations related to trocar placements with a multifilament absorbable suture. A Bartelf Macroporus mesh, cut to size in a diamond configuration, is placed and fixed, demonstrating the extent of the dissection performed.

The surgeon extends the mesh to cover the subdiaphragmatic dissection, applying HisSeal manually for better performance in minimally invasive settings. The mesh is spread to ensure adequate coverage, completing the procedure without complications.
There you go. A robotic TAR with some of the more straightforward elements of that dissection highlighted here. Thank you.
